_readme: Eval set for server/llm.js generateRoadmap(). Each case's themeGroups is a list of synonym groups; the generated agencies+flags+risks+narrative text must contain at least one keyword from EVERY group to pass. These are deliberately well-known, stable, nationally-documented facts (not obscure local minutiae) so a human can sanity-check the rubric itself. Run: node server/eval/run.js

cases.description: Building a new 15x30 inground pool with a fence, about 10 feet from the property line
cases.notes: Anchor: FL state law requires a compliant pool barrier before water is introduced (FL Statute 515 / FBC Ch. 454). High water table is a well-documented Broward-area concern.

cases.description: Full roof replacement on an existing single family home, asphalt shingle to asphalt shingle
cases.notes: Anchor: Miami-Dade/Broward are High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ) — roofing products need NOA (Notice of Acceptance). Well-documented, checkable fact.

cases.description: Building a new detached accessory dwelling unit in the backyard, 600 sq ft, new construction
cases.notes: Anchor: California has a specific statewide ADU law (Gov. Code 65852.2) requiring streamlined/ministerial approval — a well-documented, checkable fact.

cases.description: Installing rooftop solar panels on an existing single family home
cases.notes: Anchor: CA fire code requires setback/pathway clearance around roof-mounted solar; utility interconnection/net metering is a standard step. Well-documented facts.

cases.description: Building a new deck attached to the house, existing single family home in the downtown area
cases.notes: Anchor: downtown Charleston is a well-documented historic district with its own architectural review board (BAR).

cases.description: Installing a new 6 foot privacy fence around the backyard
cases.notes: Baseline normal case — no exotic anchor fact, just checks a simple project still gets a sane, well-formed answer.

cases.description: Adding a new bedroom and bathroom addition to the back of an existing house, new construction
cases.notes: Anchor: Texas has no uniform statewide residential building code adoption — a correct answer should hedge that requirements vary by the specific city/county rather than state a single confident statewide rule.

cases.description: Building a new inground pool, 15x30, in the backyard of an existing single family home
cases.notes: Contrast case with #1 — AZ also requires pool barriers, but climate/soil concerns should differ from Florida's high-water-table framing.

cases.description: Adding a new room addition with a full basement foundation to an existing house
cases.notes: Anchor: cold-climate jurisdictions require footings below the frost line — a well-documented, checkable requirement for Chicago.

cases.description: Installing a new fence around a rural residential property
cases.notes: Sparse-jurisdiction case — a correct answer should hedge (small/rural counties often have minimal zoning) rather than invent confident specifics it can't know.

Deliberately vague description — tests that the model still produces a valid, appropriately-generic (not wildly over-specific) answer rather than fabricating false precision to compensate for missing detail.

cases.description: Building a new detached accessory dwelling unit in the backyard, new construction
cases.notes: Anchor: Washington State also has ADU-friendly state legislation (HB 1337) — second state-law case in a different state to check the model isn't just pattern-matching California specifically.

cases.description: Installing a residential home elevator in an existing single family house
cases.notes: Rare/'Other' trade category — checks generalization beyond the seven named trade chips rather than a hardcoded response for common trades only.

cases.description: asdlkfj rainbow sparkles nothing real whatsoever
cases.notes: Valid-sounding location, nonsense description — the model's OWN unrecognized-project judgment (independent of the client's separate geocode check) should catch this.
Nonsense location AND description reaching the model directly (bypassing the client's geocode pre-check) — tests the model as a second line of defense, not just the client-side check.
